data.title: Hong Kong’s Langham group invests in Beijing’s ‘city of the future’ Xiongan
data.description:
A hotel operator’s planned entry into Xiongan New Area southwest of Beijing highlights a tentative but growing alignment between Hong Kong’s private sector and the state-backed development project, as leaders renew efforts to accelerate the zone’s transformation into a hi-tech growth hub.
Hong Kong-based Langham Hospitality Group, a wholly owned subsidiary of Great Eagle Holdings, said on Thursday that it had signed a management agreement with China Xiongan Group Public Service Management to open two hotels in Xiongan, located in Hebei province about 100km (62 miles) from Beijing.
The hotel complex, one of the most tangible investments by a Hong Kong company in the project, will span about 23,700 square metres (about 255,000 sq ft) and offer 300 hotel rooms under the Cordis and Ying’nFlo brands. It is scheduled to open in the second quarter of 2028.
“As the city takes shape, we believe our dual-brand approach will be well-suited to support the broad range of travel needs that will accompany its growth,” said Bob van den Oord, CEO at Langham.
The move comes after President Xi Jinping visited Xiongan last month and called for faster progress in building it into “an innovation hub in the new era” and a model for high-quality development, according to state media.
Xi reiterated that the zone’s core role was to absorb non-essential functions from Beijing, including state firms, universities and hospitals, while fostering emerging industries and improving public services.
First announced in 2017, Xiongan is a centrepiece of China’s regional strategy to ease pressure on the capital and rebalance growth across the Beijing–Tianjin–Hebei region.
The opening of the Beijing-Xiongan Intercity Railway has cut travel time from Beijing to Xiongan to around 30 minutes.
The project has faced scrutiny over slow population growth and limited commercial activity, but recent relocations – including major state-owned enterprises – signal renewed momentum.
Hong Kong’s involvement remains modest, but is taking shape across both corporate and policy channels.

Hong Kong Cyberport has proposed a “dual circulation” collaboration model with Xiongan, aimed at pairing the mainland city’s research and development capabilities with Hong Kong’s financing and international market access.
At the government level, Hong Kong has engaged Xiongan through official and business delegations and academic exchanges, focusing on infrastructure planning and city-university integration.
The visits are closely tied to Hong Kong’s own Northern Metropolis project, a planned economic and innovation hub near the mainland border, where officials are seeking to replicate elements of Xiongan’s state-led planning approach.
Hong Kong authorities have also encouraged companies from Xiongan and Beijing to use the city as a platform for overseas expansion, positioning it as a superconnector linking mainland innovation with global capital.

data.title: Hong Kong jobless rate dips for second successive quarter
data.description:
Hong Kong’s unemployment rate fell by 0.1 percentage point to 3.7 per cent in the first quarter, marking a second consecutive decline across rolling three-month periods.
The Census and Statistics Department said on Thursday the jobless rate stood at 3.7 per cent for January to March, down from 3.8 per cent in the preceding period from December to February.
The underemployment rate also edged down by 0.1 percentage point, from 1.7 per cent to 1.6 per cent over the same periods.
The accommodation services sector recorded a more notable drop in unemployment, while the foundation and superstructure sector saw an improvement in underemployment, according to the department.
Secretary for Labour and Welfare Chris Sun Yuk-han said the labour force and total employment “decreased slightly”.
“The sustained growth of the Hong Kong economy should underpin the overall labour market,” Sun said. “The government will continue to closely monitor the developments in geopolitical tensions and assess the potential implications for the labour market.”
Total employment declined by around 7,300 to 3.65 million in the first quarter and the city’s labour force also shrank by around 5,300 to 3.79 million.
The number of unemployed people – not seasonally adjusted – rose by 1,900 to 136,000 while the number of underemployed fell by 3,300, from 63,400 in the preceding three months to 60,100.
Since July last year, the unemployment rate hovered at around 3.8 to 3.9 per cent.

data.title: Hong Kong fire services declined Tai Po alert over confusion fears, inquiry hears
data.description:
Hong Kong’s fire services declined to activate an emergency alert during the city’s deadliest inferno in decades, citing concerns that it might have caused confusion among trapped residents, a public inquiry has heard.
An independent committee investigating last year’s disaster at Wang Fuk Court was also told on Thursday that the system, which would have triggered a loud, high-pitched alarm on mobile phones, would have offered little help to the Fire Services Department’s evacuation efforts, as it would take up to an hour to activate.
The committee was examining failures linked to the deactivation of fire alarms across the eight towers at the Tai Po residential complex when the blaze broke out on November 26 last year.
The inferno that lasted for around 43 hours and ravaged all but one block was the deadliest in Hong Kong since 1948, killing 168 people and displacing nearly 5,000.
The inquiry earlier heard that a property management employee at Wang Fuk Court had switched off the estate’s fire hosepipes to facilitate water tank repairs during a HK$336 million (US$43 million) renovation project and inadvertently disabled the fire alarms altogether.
Victor Dawes, the committee’s leading counsel, highlighted that five of seven affected towers caught fire at a relatively late stage, suggesting there might have been enough time to evacuate trapped residents.




Derek Armstrong Chan, a deputy fire services director for operations, said the government’s emergency alert system was “unsuitable for this incident”, as the situation required targeted one-on-one communication with residents in affected buildings rather than a citywide broadcast.
Dawes questioned that reasoning, saying it appeared to suggest the alert could not be used in scenarios such as a bomb attack unless multiple explosives were found across the city.
Chan also cited the risk of unpredictable responses due to a lack of public education and the absence of pre-prepared messages.
“In this situation, given the conditions of the burning buildings, residents leaving their flats could be more dangerous. We couldn’t anticipate how they would respond,” he said.
The witness said that activating the warning system would take up to an hour, and that the department’s existing callback mechanism would more effectively help officers locate residents.


But Dawes noted that the estimated time for activation was, in fact, for necessary administrative procedures, but the system was technically ready for immediate use.
“In light of the unprecedented incident, and considering the top priority was to alert residents of the danger, was it rather unwise if we declined to use [the emergency alert system] simply for procedural reasons?” Dawes asked.
Chan disagreed, saying the alarm might prompt residents to make poor decisions.
“Can everyone do it right in an emergency? If so, we’d never have had to put so many resources into public education,” he added.
The deputy director, who oversaw a substantial part of the operation at Wang Fuk Court, said he prioritised deploying firefighters to contain the blaze at Wang Cheong House and Wang Tai House, the first two buildings affected, while relying on police to evacuate residents from the other towers.
While the department had sufficient resources to combat the fire, delays in officers arriving meant firefighters could not conduct the two tasks simultaneously in the early hours of the blaze, Chan said.
But he accepted that the level-four fire alarm level should have been raised earlier for more firefighters and equipment to arrive sooner.
The level-four alarm was in force at 3.34pm on November 26, nearly an hour after the fire started. The department raised the alarm further to level five at 6.22pm that evening.

Thursday’s session also delved into the circumstances of the death of firefighter Ho Wai-ho, who was believed to have fallen to his death from the 31st floor of Wang Tai House during a rescue operation on November 26.
Tung Wing-kei, a divisional officer overseeing firefighters’ breathing apparatus, said Ho’s gas cylinder was likely to have been left in a pool of water on the building’s rooftop for more than 17 hours before it was discovered.
A subsequent investigation found the cylinder’s valve and its connected handwheel sustained “very rare” damage in the fire, possibly caused by a strong, blunt force.
The department was unable to pinpoint the cause as the device’s digital log was water-damaged beyond repair.
The hearing continues at City Gallery in Central on Friday.

data.title: AI-driven Hong Kong stock inflows from mainland China slow as options multiply
data.description:
Mainland Chinese investors have slowed their purchases of Hong Kong-listed shares this year after last year’s record inflows, as more artificial intelligence investment opportunities have emerged in mainland markets, according to BNP Paribas.
Southbound inflows via the Stock Connect cross-border system have reached about US$30 billion so far this year, a slower pace than 2025, when they hit US$180 billion for the full year, according to the French bank. The deceleration reflected changing market dynamics rather than a retreat from Chinese assets, BNP Paribas strategists said at a media briefing on Wednesday.
“Investors are still positive on China’s AI story,” said Jason Lui, head of Asia-Pacific equity and derivative strategy, adding that the difference this year was that they had “more options to express those views”.
Last year’s rally was driven by the sudden emergence of AI start-up DeepSeek, which caught markets by surprise and led to concentrated buying of Hong Kong-listed technology giants, he said. This year had no event of similar magnitude, while a wave of new listings over the past six to nine months had provided more pure-play AI investment options across both mainland and Hong Kong markets.
As a result, investors were increasingly shifting from broad index exposure to more selective bets on individual companies, reducing the need to rely on Hong Kong-listed internet stocks as proxies for the AI theme, according to the bank.
The slowdown in southbound inflows also reflected a higher base after last year’s surge, said Kenny Tang Sing-hing, chairman of the Hong Kong Institute of Financial Analysts and Professional Commentators.
The shift was closely tied to the AI investment theme, as many of the most sought-after AI and chip companies had already secured funding from mainland cornerstone and strategic investors during their pre-IPO stages.
“A lot of the capital has already gone into these companies before they list,” Tang said, noting that new shares were not accessible through the Stock Connect programme.
Despite weaker southbound inflows, trading activity in Hong Kong has remained resilient. The average daily turnover in March rose 8 per cent from a year earlier to HK$304 billion (US$39 billion), while the total market capitalisation of Hong Kong’s securities market reached HK$45.9 trillion at the end of March, a 15 per cent increase from a year earlier, stock exchange data showed.
The AI investment cycle continued to underpin the global equity market, according to BNP Paribas. Technology stocks, particularly those linked to hardware and supply chains, regained momentum in recent months, supported by strong pricing power and resilience to inflationary pressures, the bank added.
It also highlighted improving opportunities in mainland markets, particularly among mid-cap stocks. The CSI 500 index, which has a higher weighting in sectors such as technology, industrials and materials, was expected to benefit from Beijing’s push for technology improvement under the 15th five-year plan, the bank said.
Mid-cap stocks tended to be more sensitive to changes in producer prices, and a gradual recovery in the producer price index could improve their earnings outlook, BNP Paribas said. It added that the CSI 500 offered a blend of exposure to both technology and cyclical recovery, positioning it well in the current environment.

data.title: Personal info of 9,045 people compromised in Hong Kong private club data breach
data.description:
A data breach at Yau Yat Chuen Garden City Club has compromised the personal information of more than 9,000 people, Hong Kong’s privacy watchdog has found, urging organisations to review security measures and update software to close loopholes.
The Office of the Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data on Thursday also released a guide for parents and teachers to safeguard children’s online privacy amid increasing risks facing youngsters online.
Revealing the data breach, Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data Ada Chung Lai-ling said the private recreational club notified the office on October 31 last year that its club management system was rendered inoperable due to a ransomware attack that encrypted information system’s files stored on a server.
The management system was provided and maintained by an external service provider, which was able to remotely access the server via dedicated software to offer technical support.
The office’s investigation revealed that the software was operating on an outdated version that contained a known security vulnerability at the time of the data breach, enabling the “threat actor” to compromise the account credentials used by the service provider and gain direct entry to the server where personal data was stored.
The server was also left in a logged-in state without the implementation of additional authentication controls, while the club’s antivirus software and firewall were outdated, making them unable to detect and prevent hacking activities.
The breach affected 9,045 people, including active and former members and supplementary cardholders of the club, a private, non-profit social and recreational organisation.
The compromised personal data included full names, Hong Kong identity card numbers, passport numbers, dates of birth, email addresses, contact numbers and addresses.
Chung said the club was found to have several deficiencies that led to the breach, including using outdated remote access software with a security vulnerability, lacking user authentication measures for remote access to the server, and using outdated antivirus software and firewall.
It also lacked organisational measures for information security and retained personal data for a prolonged period of time, she said.
“Membership and customer data is a valuable asset of an organisation and can be said to be a high-risk target of cyberattacks,” she said.
“Organisations should adopt a proactive strategy, regularly review the effectiveness of the security measures of their information systems, and allocate sufficient resources to protect such personal data.”

Chung said the club had contravened the Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance and had been served an enforcement notice requiring it to take measures to remedy the breach and prevent similar contraventions in the future.
She also recommended organisations take steps to protect their information systems, such as updating software and firewalls in a timely manner, implementing effective user authentication for data access and conducting regular security risk assessments.
Separately, the watchdog also released practical tips for parents and teachers to safeguard children’s online privacy.
“As children’s cognitive abilities and physical and mental health are more vulnerable than adults, their privacy needs to be protected,” Chung said.
The guide suggested parents and teachers join in children’s online activities, including discussing with youngsters the things to be aware of online and using parental controls provided by online platforms to monitor children’s activities.
It called for the safeguarding of children’s online privacy, including reminding youngsters not to excessively share personal data when using online platforms or interacting with artificial intelligence (AI) tools, while parents and teachers should review and change default privacy settings.
Parents and teachers should become role models by setting good examples, protecting their own personal data and respecting others’ data privacy, the guide said.
It also suggested that children should be reminded of risks such as online scams, cyberbullying, abuse of AI deepfakes and doxxing.

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest 9 over HK$1.7 million in fake travel insurance claims
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested nine people over their alleged involvement in a travel insurance fraud syndicate that staged overseas accidents to claim more than HK$1.7 million (US$217,053).
The force said on Thursday it had received a report from the Hong Kong Federation of Insurers in October concerning suspicious travel insurance claims, ranging from “a few thousand to tens of thousands of Hong Kong dollars”.
“Police analysed more than 70 suspicious claim cases and discovered that these claimants submitted various forged documents, including air tickets, hotel receipts and medical records from overseas hospitals,” said senior inspector Lam Ho-lim of the commercial crime bureau.
“This was done to create a false impression of being injured while travelling abroad, despite the fact that they had never actually left Hong Kong, to claim medical expenses from insurance companies.”
On Wednesday, officers arrested nine Hong Kong residents on suspicion of fraud and conspiracy to defraud. The group comprised six men and three women aged between 25 and 68.
Among those arrested were seven claimants and two alleged syndicate members, including individuals identified as both current and former insurance agents.
Police said the suspects falsely claimed to have travelled to countries including Vietnam, Japan, Italy and Singapore multiple times between April and November last year.
“They sought medical treatment at the same overseas hospitals for similar injuries and symptoms to make these suspicious claims to insurance companies. The total amount involved in these claims reached HK$1.7 million,” Lam said.

He added that the syndicate operated by providing claimants with specific phone numbers and instructing them to open bank accounts for payouts before the proceeds were withdrawn in cash from automated teller machines.
Chief Inspector Ho Yun-yin of the bureau said the force would continue to investigate and did not rule out further arrests.
“Residents should not assume that insurance companies lack the capability to investigate or will not report to the police simply because the claim amounts are small or the fabricated accidents occurred overseas,” he said.
“The force maintains a close and continuous partnership with the Hong Kong Federation of Insurers and the insurance industry.”
He urged the public not to be swayed or recruited by ill-intentioned individuals out of greed, warning they could be exploited as puppets in a fraud syndicate.
In Hong Kong, both fraud and conspiracy to defraud carry a maximum penalty of 14 years’ imprisonment.

data.title: Hong Kong MTR unveils HK$300 million wheelset centre, boosting efficiency by 80%
data.description:
Hong Kong’s MTR Corporation has unveiled its first wheelset maintenance centre, set to open in the coming months, cutting the time for the labour-intensive process from more than three days to 10 hours.
The rail giant said on Thursday that the new HK$300 million (US$38.3 million) Integrated Wheelset Maintenance Centre, located at the Ho Man Tin siding, would be capable of processing 1,500 wheelsets annually through automation.
The capacity is designed to meet growing service demands on the Tuen Ma and East Rail lines.
Lee Kim-hung, the MTR Corp’s deputy director of operations engineering maintenance, said the facility represented a comprehensive overhaul of traditional maintenance workflows through the use of cutting-edge technology, while enhancing safety.
“Even when our wheels show just a few millimetres of wear and tear, we perform grinding to restore their circular shape. Based on the condition and service life of each wheelset, we also carry out full replacements every few years,” he said.
He explained that wheelset repair and replacement were some of the most repetitive and labour-intensive tasks in railway maintenance, noting that a single train carriage contains four 1.5-tonne wheelsets, each comprising two wheels and an axle.
“Our goal is to speed up processes, improve effectiveness and boost productivity. The maintenance time for each wheelset will be significantly reduced from the current three to four working days to approximately 10 hours,” Lee said.
He added that the company expected to increase productivity by about 80 per cent.
“While traditional wheelset maintenance typically requires more than 30 engineering staff members, the centre can be operated by just six employees who focus on remote monitoring and data analysis,” he said.
The new facility forms part of the corporation’s HK$65 billion five-year programme to repair and maintain its railway assets through 2027.
The centre offers a one-stop automated process in four stages, starting with the dismantling of wheelsets due for replacement.
Once placed on a rack and assigned priority through the management system, specialised robotic arms move the components to a replacement station.

Cleaning robots then wash the axle while the system prepares new wheels for assembly. This is followed by water-jet cleaning and magnetic particle testing of the axle to ensure structural integrity.
After the new wheels are fitted, the completed component undergoes ultrasonic testing and a simulated high-speed run to ensure smooth operation before being returned to the depot for reinstallation.
Lee said the automation was managed by three digital systems to enhance speed and safety, allowing engineers to remotely track each wheelset’s real-time progress through the maintenance cycle.
He added that the systems coordinated eight custom robotic arms to move 1.5-tonne components, while integrating machinery status for centralised monitoring.
He also said safety gates were linked to the system, immediately halting all robotic operations if opened.
Data from the centre will be integrated into MTR systems, enabling analysis of wheelset wear patterns to inform long-term maintenance strategies.
Lee said the facility would serve the Tuen Ma and East Rail lines, which are expected to require about 900 wheelset changes a year after the cross-harbour extension opened in 2022. He added that the corporation built in buffer capacity when designing the centre.
“We hope to reach a maximum of 1,500 sets. As we continue with commissioning and testing, we can identify other areas for optimisation and see how we can make the process even more efficient,” he said.
Lee said the corporation would monitor operations to determine whether to extend the centre’s services to other MTR lines, and what workflow or equipment adjustments might be needed.

data.title: Why no disaster alarm was raised in Tai Po fire, probe hears
data.description:
Wednesday’s hearing also covered wooden movable boards installed on the buildings’ emergency staircases that replaced fireproof windows. Preliminary investigations showed that the boards caused smoke to enter the building after the fire broke out, posing a danger to residents and firefighters.
Yung said even if fire service officers found such installations during inspections, they would not directly intervene as they lacked professional knowledge about engineering. The matter should be handled by “the most professional” departments, he insisted.
But Dawes argued that the department should be in a position to handle the issue despite the supposed division of labour, citing its duty to “give advice on fire protection measures and fire hazards as occasion requires” under the law.

data.title: WeWork finds new lease of life in Hong Kong as uncertainty drives global demand
data.description:
Global flexible workspace operator WeWork is expanding in Hong Kong as companies scale back long-term office commitments, opening its fifth site in Causeway Bay to tap rising demand for short-term, flexible space amid economic and geopolitical uncertainty.
The new location at Soundwill Plaza II Midtown, owned by Soundwill Holdings, was previously occupied by another co-working operator. WeWork secured the site after competing with several rivals.
“In the current uncertain market outlook, landlords are looking for reliable, long-term and stable partners,” said Wang Cheung, city leader for WeWork Hong Kong and Taipei. The firm now operates four floors in the building, totalling 31,800 sq ft.
“We believe occupancy at this location will reach around 80 per cent by the end of the year,” Cheung said.
The opening reflects WeWork’s gradual rebuild in the city. The company re-entered Hong Kong in 2022 after closing several locations in 2020, and has since expanded across Hong Kong Island while eyeing new sites in Kowloon.
The Causeway Bay project targets small teams, offering more than 60 offices for groups of two to 13 people. Early demand has come from mainland Chinese firms establishing initial overseas operations. Monthly pricing starts at HK$2,800 (US$358) for hot desks and HK$4,000 per workstation for private offices.

The expansion aligns with a broader shift in demand. Data from The Instant Group shows flexible workspace demand in Hong Kong rose 27 per cent in the first two months of 2026, as companies responded to “ongoing economic uncertainty” by prioritising flexibility.
The same data showed demand was increasingly concentrated in smaller offices, particularly in the one- to nine-desk range, reflecting hybrid work and more distributed teams. Core districts continue to dominate, with Central and Admiralty accounting for about a third of demand, followed by Causeway Bay and Wan Chai.
Within Causeway Bay, WeWork’s Lee Garden One site, owned by Hysan Development, is its best-performing location, with occupancy at 92 per cent, supported by tenants across technology, finance and professional services.
WeWork said global factors were also shaping demand. “Amid ongoing geopolitical instability in the Middle East and globally, we have seen capital inflow shifting towards Hong Kong as funds seek a safe harbour,” the company said.
These trends are playing out against an uneven office market recovery.
According to JLL, overall grade A vacancy stood at 13.5 per cent at the end of March, with Central at 9.6 per cent and Wan Chai and Causeway Bay at 10.3 per cent, compared with 20.4 per cent in Kowloon East.
Rents edged up 0.1 per cent month on month in March, driven largely by Central, while Kowloon East continued to face pressure, with rents falling 0.7 per cent.
Flexible workspace pricing has remained relatively stable despite the broader downturn. Average desk rates rose about 2 per cent year on year to around US$608 per month in 2025, according to The Instant Group.
Landlords are adapting. Hysan Development has partnered with co-working operators in Causeway Bay, while CK Asset Holdings and Hang Lung Properties have launched in-house flexible workspace platforms to support occupancy.

data.title: Alibaba opens Qwen app to partners with China Eastern Airlines tie-up
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding has struck its first external partnership for its flagship consumer artificial intelligence app, linking it with China Eastern Airlines in a move that pushes its agentic capabilities beyond the company’s own ecosystem and into real-world services.
The company said the fresh collaboration allowed users of the Qwen app to manage the full flight booking process – from search and ticket purchase to seat selection and check-in – within a single natural-language chat interface. It also acted as a proactive intelligent companion, anticipating user needs and suggesting options, it added.
The Qwen app, Alibaba said, would expand into airline loyalty services and other travel-related offerings, adding partners both within and beyond Alibaba’s ecosystem to deepen real-world use cases and strengthen its position in applied AI. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
“Expanding from the strong momentum within the Alibaba ecosystem, integrating China Eastern marks the first time our agentic capabilities are available to external partners, making everyday life easier for even more users with new use cases,” said Wu Jia, president of Qwen app.
The deal underscores the e-commerce and AI giant’s effort to push forward adoption of its AI stack – spanning infrastructure, cloud services, foundation models and both enterprise and consumer-facing applications – to justify its heavy investments.
Alibaba in early 2025 pledged to invest 380 billion yuan (US$55.6 billion) in AI and cloud over three years, and has already spent more than 120 billion yuan in the sector in the four quarters through September.
Alibaba aimed to generate US$100 billion in annual external revenue from its combined cloud and AI businesses within five years, top executives said in March.
Qwen app, which was given a human face this week after Alibaba unveiled a dimpled girl as its digital avatar – echoing a similar move by ByteDance’s Doubao – has been integrated across Alibaba’s ecosystem including with Taobao, Alipay, delivery app Shangou, Amap and ticketing platform Damai.

Alibaba said its AI-driven shopping features had attracted more than 140 million users by February, while AI-assisted travel bookings during the Chinese New Year holiday surged more than eightfold, pointing to rising demand for AI-enabled services.
Pivoting to AI, Alibaba in March established the Alibaba Token Hub (ATH) Business Group, which brings all its core AI teams and products together under one umbrella. The business group is built to drive the creation, delivery and application of tokens, the basic units of data in AI applications.
In April, it set up a high-level technology committee led by CEO Eddie Wu Yongming, to streamline operations around AI.

data.title: Film on Manila hostage crisis sparks debate over ‘secondary trauma’ in Hong Kong
data.description:
A Hong Kong film adapted from the 2010 Manila hostage crisis, which led to the deaths of eight Hongkongers, has stirred online debate, with some questioning whether it could cause “secondary trauma” and others saying it offers lessons from the past.
Titled Beyond Hostage Crisis, the film was released in Malaysia in April and is set to open in Hong Kong on May 28.
Information about the 90-minute film starring newly crowned Hong Kong Film Awards best actress Fish Liew Chi-yu is available on the MCL Cinemas website.
The plot is based on the August 23, 2010 hostage crisis, in which a 55-year-old former police officer armed with an M-16 rifle hijacked a tour bus carrying 22 Hong Kong tourists and three Filipinos in Manila.
He demanded reinstatement and later released nine people during negotiations, including two Filipinos.
After a 12-hour siege, seven Hong Kong tourists and the tour guide were killed, while seven Hongkongers survived. The gunman was later found dead with a gunshot wound to the head.
The incident deeply shook Hongkongers, many of whom watched it unfold live on television.

On social media platform Threads, users expressed mixed reactions to the film’s release.
“I watched it all on television back then – why would I want to watch it again?” one user said, followed by a string of crying emojis.
“I will remember that [incident] for the rest of my life, from the moment when the hostages could still open the curtains to peek out of the window, and the tour guide was still looking around at the back of the bus … I truly believed they would be OK, and then the gunshots, the screams …”
Another user lashed out at the film.
“Do they think Hongkongers have not suffered enough? We have not healed from the Wang Fuk Court tragedy yet, and now they want us to relive the hostage crisis all over again? This is insane,” the user said in a post, referring to a Tai Po housing complex where 168 people were killed in one of Hong Kong’s deadliest fires in November.
A third Threads user also questioned the film: “Will it cause secondary trauma to survivors and family members?”
But some users defended the film.
“I believe the film has its value – to let the next generation who did not live through it know that this event actually happened,” a Threads user named “kena280” said.
Another user noted that many wars, collective traumas and earthquake disasters had also been made into films.
“The focus should be on honouring the victims and learning from the past, rather than being too sensitive and calling it a form of secondary trauma,” another user with the handle “dordor.neko” wrote.
Yik Siu-ling, a survivor injured in the incident, praised the film in a March interview posted on Facebook.
“It is a really good film, very touching. Especially under Hong Kong’s current climate, this film is really worth watching,” Yik said in Cantonese.
The film has been screening in Malaysia since early April.
Times Multimedia Innovation Limited was one of the production companies. According to the government’s Cultural and Creative Industries Development Agency website, the company’s contact person shares the same Chinese name as the film’s director, Tony Leung Hung-wah.
The South China Morning Post has reached out to Times Multimedia Innovation for comment.

data.title: 600 families seek second visit as worst-hit Wang Fuk Court tower reopens
data.description:
About 600 families from Hong Kong’s fire-ravaged Wang Fuk Court have requested a second visit to their flats, authorities have said, as residents of the most severely damaged tower return to salvage their belongings.
Deputy Chief Secretary for Administration Cheuk Wing-hing said on Thursday that the government would provide additional support for residents of Wang Cheong House, the hardest-hit tower where 81 of the 168 deaths in the Tai Po fire occurred. Residents are returning between April 23 and April 28.
“Wang Cheong House and Wang Tai House were severely damaged by the fire, with more fatalities reported. Returning residents will need greater support, particularly emotional and psychological assistance,” he said.
Households of Wang Tai House, which recorded the highest death toll at 82, will return between April 29 and May 4.

Cheuk said each resident entering the block would be accompanied by a police officer, noting that the Social Welfare Department would deploy a psychologist and a social worker to support households where fatalities occurred.
He added that civil servants, police officers and Civil Aid Service members stationed on each floor would be ready to provide assistance at any time.
Only five floors of Wang Cheong House and Wang Tai House will be opened each day over the six-day period.
Some residents returned in the morning to Wang Cheong House and Wang Yan House carrying suitcases, nylon bags, backpacks and large cardboard boxes, hoping to retrieve as many valuables as possible.
Cheuk also said the government had received requests from about 600 families across the seven blocks as of Wednesday, up from the 400 he announced earlier.
The increase follows residents of Wang Sun House returning over the past three days to collect their belongings.
“We will proactively arrange for households to retrieve their belongings again,” he said, urging those in need to contact their assigned social worker.

Residents of Wang Yan House will also return to their homes between Thursday and Saturday.
Wang Cheong House was the most severely damaged block among the eight towers at Wang Fuk Court, with 63 per cent of the flats gutted.
The fire broke out in a lightwell outside flats 104 and 105 at around 2.42pm on November 26, 2025. Cigarette butts were later found at the scene and on the scaffolding-covered ground below.
The fire engulfed the 31-storey tower in under 20 minutes, then swept through six other blocks rapidly within 85 minutes.
Wang Tai House, the second block hit, recorded 82 deaths – the highest toll.
Speaking on Thursday, Secretary for Security Chris Tang Ping-keung said police had used exoskeletons for site cleaning, adding that officers had to master skills and undergo training.
“As we cannot be sure whether residents, especially the elderly, can use it safely, and as it poses certain risks, the government did not provide the equipment,” he said, adding that on-site personnel would also monitor residents using exoskeletons.

AidVengers, a charity that previously offered to lend exoskeletons to Wang Fuk Court residents, has cancelled the service due to unpredictable risks.
Under the 15-day programme that started on Monday, Wang Fuk Court residents are allowed three hours in their flats, excluding time spent on the stairs.
Up to four people per household may return at a time, but only one or two are allowed to enter flats that are severely damaged and deemed unsafe.
About 6,000 residents registered with the government to visit their former homes and collect belongings.
The inferno that tore through seven of the eight blocks of Wang Fuk Court killed 168 people, including a firefighter.

data.title: Exclusive|Travel scheme could allow Hong Kong, Macau yachts to sail to 6 bay area ports
data.description:
A long-awaited cross-border solo travel scheme will allow yachts from Macau and Hong Kong to easily sail to about six designated ports in the western waters off the Greater Bay Area, with the policy to be finalised as early as this summer, the South China Morning Post has learned.
A Guangdong provincial government document, seen by the SCMP, also showed several routes that were recommended for leisure, such as the waters off Castle Peak Bay, the Pearl River Delta and Fan Lau Kok of the Lantau Channel.
Under the proposal, leisure yachting tours would be allowed in the waters around the Wanshan islands, which are southwest of Hong Kong, and the smaller islands at bays such as Baicaowan, Nanwan, Luanshi Bay and Putouwan to the south of the city.
The document also suggested that yachts with local licences could obtain a temporary mainland vessel nationality certificate to operate multiple times in designated areas for 180 days.
The proposed guidelines limited applicants strictly to Hong Kong and Macau residents holding mainland travel permits in the initial stage, excluding foreigners who hold home-return permits.
A source said that the policy might be implemented as early as this summer – more than a decade after the idea first emerged.


In his latest policy address last October, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu identified the scheme as a way to develop a high-spending yacht economy.
“Hong Kong and Macau yachts should enter and exit from designated ports, and carry out water leisure activities within the delineated water areas,” the draft document said.
Under the draft proposal, vessels entering mainland waters will have to restrict their leisure activities to specific locations in archipelagos within the Pearl River Delta and Shenzhen Bay.
But authorities would retain the power to dynamically adjust these approved leisure zones.
According to the document, Guangzhou Nansha passenger port, Shenzhen Shekou cruise homeport, Shenzhen airport ferry terminal, Zhuhai Wanshan port, Zhuhai Jiuzhou port and Zhongshan port were among the six designated entry points opened up in the initial roll-out of the policy.
The government would also implement an exemption from needing a guarantee for eligible incoming vessels and offer expedited border clearance channels that allowed passengers to return directly to their yachts after inspection, the document added.
The scheme strictly prohibited yachts from transporting cargo or engaging in transfers, mortgages or alternative uses after entering the mainland.
Any participants who submitted false materials for their certificates would face immediate revocation and a three-year ban from reapplying.
The draft also stated that yachts would be required to maintain active automatic identification systems (AIS), while owners would need to sign management agreements with recognised mainland clubs to ensure safety and pollution control protocols.
AIS transponders are designed to automatically provide position, identification and other information about the ship to coastal authorities and other vessels.

Lee Shu-kam, head of Hong Kong Shue Yan University’s economics and finance department, said that the proposed solo travel scheme’s highly restricted, one-way nature would yield minimal economic benefits for the city in the short term, with local wealth primarily flowing across the border.
The current framework could serve primarily as a controlled pilot scheme for testing cross-border monitoring mechanisms before potentially easing regulations for international participants, he added.
Lee said that to boost the policy’s economic benefit to Hong Kong, authorities could amend it by restricting it as an incentive for investment or to open a family office, so that yacht owners could return to the mainland via Hong Kong.
Opening the maritime borders to foreign-owned vessels and overseas residents in the future could act as a powerful draw to attract wealthy individuals to the city to establish family offices, the academic said.
Such an expansion would drive sustained demand for highly specialised professional services across the business sector, including legal, financial, marine insurance and yacht maintenance, Lee added.
According to the Airport Authority Hong Kong, the value of the global yachting market is projected to reach US$45 billion by 2032.
The authority said the city currently had 12,500 licensed yachts, which competed for only 4,300 berths, necessitating new infrastructure such as the statutory body’s recently launched “Skytopia” marina.

data.title: Families seek review of coroner’s findings in Lamma ferry disaster inquest
data.description:
Families of victims who died in Hong Kong’s Lamma ferry disaster 13 years ago have filed an application for a judicial review against a coroner’s findings in an inquest, saying the conclusions showed irrationality and failed to weigh contradictory evidence.
Four individuals lodged the application in the High Court on Wednesday. They acted without lawyers.
They want the court to quash the coroner’s findings that a missing watertight door on the ill-fated Lamma IV ferry was a deliberate design choice, not an oversight. They are also seeking a fresh inquest on that issue.
Thirty-nine people died in the disaster on October 1, 2012, when the Lamma IV was hit by the catamaran Sea Smooth near Lamma Island, making it Hong Kong’s deadliest maritime accident in decades.
Lamma IV was carrying 124 HK Electric employees and their relatives to watch the National Day fireworks over Victoria Harbour.


The application names the coroner as respondent. The city’s director of marine, Cheoy Lee Shipyard, HK Electric and Hong Kong and Kowloon Ferry, operator of the Sea Smooth, are listed as interested parties.
The 44-day inquest held last year returned a verdict of unlawful killing of the 39 in January, pointing to gross negligence by the ships’ two coxswains, while ruling on issues relating to the circumstances of the crash.
Coroner Monica Chow Wai-choo ruled that the omission of a watertight door on the underdeck of the sunken Lamma IV was deliberate and compliant with regulatory requirements when the vessel was built in 1994 and 1995.
But some victims’ relatives had earlier argued that the absence of a watertight door was the main reason for the ship’s rapid sinking and the tragic loss of lives, citing marine experts’ unanimous opinion that the door would have allowed the ship to stay afloat until rescuers arrived.

The judicial review applicants said in their submission that the coroner did not consider matters which she was bound to and failed to exclude from her consideration matters which were irrelevant.
They argued the coroner ignored contradictions in witness testimony, wrongly treated the evidence of two key witnesses, Ken Lo Ngok-yang and John Lim, as corroborative, and overlooked drafting patterns in the ship’s plans.
Lo, a director of Cheoy Lee Shipyards, oversaw the building of Lamma IV and testified that leaving out a watertight door was a conscious design choice.
Lim, a draughtsman involved in preparing the vessel’s plans, gave evidence that the coroner treated as corroborative of Lo’s account.
The coroner’s inquest was held after two relatives of victims convinced the Court of Appeal that it was in the public interest to investigate the incident once again, even though the causes of the tragedy had been largely identified in previous inquiries.
The four applying for the judicial review are Alice Leung Shuk-ling and Leung Ka-yan, both next of kin of victim Pieta Leung Ka-kit, Philip Chiu Ping-chuen, who lost his elder sister Chiu Siu-king in the disaster, and Tsui Keung, next of kin of victim Tsui Chi-wai.

data.title: Can ‘token exports’ give China an edge in the AI era?
data.description:
As Chinese artificial intelligence companies continue to push for the adoption of their flagship models overseas, they have taken on a new role as a key source of so-called “token exports” to the global market.
Chinese AI models accounted for four of the top 10 models in terms of token consumption on popular AI model marketplace OpenRouter from March 18 to April 18, highlighting their growing visibility in global developer usage of AI systems.
Domestic token demand has been surging at an exponential pace. Data from China’s National Data Administration showed that daily token consumption had exceeded 140 trillion by March 2026, a more than 1,000-fold increase from 100 billion in early 2024.
The figure also rose over 40 per cent in the first three months of this year compared with levels at the end of 2025.
The South China Morning Post examines the evolving compute ecosystem and how China’s token-driven AI services are expanding internationally, albeit with structural limitations.
What does OpenRouter data reveal?
OpenRouter, a US-based model aggregation platform, integrates more than 300 leading AI models and processes over 30 trillion tokens per month.
It accounts for roughly 1 per cent of all trackable global token consumption, according to research by Frank Nagle of the MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy.
The platform provides a unified application programming interface (API), allowing developers to switch between models and underlying compute providers in real time depending on cost, performance and use case.
Through OpenRouter, global developers can access and deploy models across borders, with inference often routed to distributed cloud data centres.
OpenRouter data also shows that the most token-intensive applications are increasingly driven by autonomous agent systems such as OpenClaw, alongside code-generation tools such as Claude Code used by individual developers.
However, it should be noted that high rankings on OpenRouter do not necessarily translate into proportional revenue or global dominance, as the platform captures only a small fraction of global token usage and excludes large volumes generated through consumer applications and enterprise systems operated by firms such as OpenAI, Anthropic and Google.

What are token exports and how do Chinese companies benefit?
The term “token export” is increasingly used to describe cross-border AI inference, where tokenised requests are processed in geographically distributed compute environments rather than locally.
Usage data from OpenRouter suggests that Chinese large language models have gained rapid traction among global developers. Before this year, the platform’s top three models by token consumption were US firms OpenAI, Anthropic and Google.
Starting this year, however, models from Chinese companies including MiniMax, Moonshot AI, Zhipu AI, StepFun, DeepSeek and Xiaomi have intermittently ranked among the global top tier in token usage.
Many Chinese models are also frequently released for free or with highly permissive pricing on OpenRouter, further boosting their token consumption share without necessarily translating into proportional revenue.
China’s API pricing is significantly lower, typically one-fifth to one-twentieth of Western rivals, according to OpenRouter data.
MiniMax M2.5 costs about US$0.3 per million tokens versus US$5 for Anthropic’s Claude Opus, while Alibaba Cloud’s Qwen 3.5 is priced at roughly US$0.11 per million tokens, around one-eighteenth of Google DeepMind’s Gemini.
“Currently, domestic companies still primarily focus on cost-effectiveness, trying to make their prices more attractive,” said Duan Bing, an analyst from Nomura.
“Their gap with the most advanced overseas models is also narrowing, so overseas users have real usage scenarios [for Chinese models],” he added.

What are the structural limits of token exports?
What is often described as “token exports” remains, in physical terms, a form of model distribution rather than true cross-border commoditisation of compute.
Xu Shan, director of international development at the Institute of AI under the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology, noted in an article published on the website of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology that OpenRouter did not fully reflect global token consumption patterns.
Xu added that many Chinese models appearing on OpenRouter were deployed via overseas cloud infrastructure, meaning usage was often hosted outside China rather than processed in domestic data centres.
China’s cost-based advantage remains structurally fragile. Most leading domestic AI firms reported 2025 revenues of between 100 million yuan (US$14.6 million) and 700 million yuan, while companies such as OpenAI and Anthropic have reached reported annualised revenues of over US$25 billion and US$30 billion respectively, highlighting a significant monetisation gap.
While Chinese models are gaining visibility through open platforms and aggressive pricing, analysts said the gap between usage share and commercial scale suggested that China’s role in the emerging token economy remained primarily one of distribution rather than full-scale economic dominance.
In other words, rising token consumption does not yet equate to control over the global AI value chain.
Nomura’s Duan, meanwhile, expected token prices to pick up when domestic models penetrated into enterprise scenarios, leading to an increase in users’ willingness to pay.
“In the future, it’s not necessarily true that large-scale token models in China will primarily be used by overseas users, domestic demand may also grow,” he said.

data.title: Tough penalties planned to stop unapproved fire system shutdowns in Hong Kong
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have proposed new offences with tougher punishments for fire installation contractors who fail to obtain prior approval before deactivating firefighting systems or do not report malfunctions within 24 hours, following the city’s deadliest blaze in decades, the South China Morning Post has learned.
Other proposed changes included drastically increasing the maximum penalty for existing offences related to the unauthorised handling of fire equipment and the issuance of false or misleading certificates, from the original HK$50,000 (US$6,380) fine to a maximum fine of HK$3 million and three years’ imprisonment.
Hearings by a judge-led independent inquiry into last November’s deadly blaze at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court exposed a series of failures and alleged malpractice by various parties, which led to the deactivation of fire alarms during renovations.
The SCMP learned on Wednesday that the Security Bureau proposed a set of legal amendments to improve oversight of fire installation contractors, including requiring prior approval before deactivating firefighting systems, such as hose reels and fire alarms.
Under existing regulations, contractors are only required to submit a shutdown notice to authorities after deactivating such systems.
Contractors would also be in breach of regulations if they failed to notify the Fire Services Department of any malfunctioning firefighting systems within 24 hours, display annual fire inspection certificates or implement temporary fire safety measures.
Those found to have breached the new laws for the first time could be fined up to HK$200,000, with an additional HK$20,000 for every further day of non-compliance.
For subsequent convictions, they could face an escalated fine of HK$400,000, with an additional daily penalty of up to HK$40,000, and a year in prison.
Another new offence will target any personnel who deliberately or recklessly misuse or interfere with fire equipment, with offenders facing a fine of HK$1 million and a one-year jail term.
But for serious cases resulting in severe casualties and property losses, the offence will carry a maximum penalty of three years’ imprisonment and a fine of HK$3 million.




The government also suggested more stringent punishments for existing offences, including unauthorised installation, inspection and repair of fire equipment by non-licensed personnel, and the signing and issuance of false or misleading certificates.
The amendment proposed a maximum penalty of a HK$3 million fine and three years’ imprisonment, up from the current HK$50,000 fine.
A source said the proposed legislation would hold property management companies legally liable for ensuring fire safety, in addition to owners’ corporations.
Liable parties failing to ensure the operability of firefighting systems or not conducting periodic inspections could face a maximum fine of HK$500,000, a tenfold increase from the current HK$50,000.
The source said the government would also introduce fixed penalties ranging from HK$1,500 to HK$6,000 for certain violations, including failure to display annual fire inspection certificates and implement the required temporary fire safety measures.
Other offences punishable by fixed penalties would include blocking or locking means of escape or failing to comply with fire hazard abatement notices.
Victor Dawes, lead counsel for the independent committee, highlighted that the failure of fire alarms across the eight blocks of Wang Fuk Court was one of the six “human factors” that contributed to the high number of deaths in the fire.
The inquiry heard in recent weeks that employees of ISS EastPoint, the estate’s property management company, who were not licensed fire services personnel, switched off the main power supply controlling the hose reel system to allow maintenance of the fire water tanks, and were unaware that this move had also deactivated the fire alarms.

China Status Development and Engineering, hired by the estate’s renovation contractor, Prestige Construction and Engineering, was found to have filed 85 shutdown notices to deactivate the fire hydrant and hose reel system across all eight blocks from as early as April 7 last year, despite failing to visit the site to assess the necessity of such a move.
Victory Fire Engineering, the contractor responsible for maintaining the estate’s fire services equipment, also failed to notify authorities when they discovered that the hose reel system and fire alarms had been turned off a week before the blaze.
Lawmaker Mark Chong Ho-fung said he expected the legal changes to better deter malpractice. He emphasised that property management companies should step up frontline inspections to ensure that firefighting systems were in place.
“For example, regarding the blockage of fire escapes, by the time firefighters discover it during inspection, the situation is already critical and has likely persisted for a long time,” he said. “But the building attendant patrols every day, and they should have spotted it.”
Chong also called on authorities to take swift and decisive enforcement action upon receiving reports of breaches.
“If you pass a law but remain loose in its enforcement, the legislation will be useless,” the lawmaker said.
The proposed amendments are set to be discussed at a Legislative Council security panel meeting on May 5.
The bureau has also proposed a series of amendments to curb illegal refuelling amid surging global oil prices by targeting buyers of illicit fuels in the city.
Under the planned legislation, offenders will face a maximum penalty of HK$1 million and one year in prison.

data.title: Hong Kong-Shenzhen tech cooperation gets a boost with Lenovo AI lab in Hetao hub
data.description:
Hong Kong’s technology cooperation with Shenzhen received a boost on Wednesday as Lenovo Group, the world’s biggest personal computer maker, set up an innovation lab at the city’s new Hetao tech hub and pledged to help firms adopt artificial intelligence and expand globally.
Lenovo is the first multinational tech firm to publicly announce its presence on the Hong Kong side of the Hetao zone – the Hong Kong-Shenzhen Innovation and Technology Park (HSITP) – which covers 87 hectares in the city’s northern Lok Ma Chau area.
Ken Wong, president of Lenovo’s Solutions & Services Group, said the new Lenovo AI Innovation Lab at HSITP aimed to be an “open platform” to help partners access the tech giant’s AI solutions and scale their businesses internationally.
The cross-border Hetao Shenzhen-Hong Kong Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Zone was launched in 2017, following Beijing’s call for closer integration between the two cities.

The Beijing and Morrisville, US, dual-headquartered PC maker chose Hetao for its new AI centre as it combined Hong Kong’s advantages in research and access to the global market with the manufacturing strengths of Shenzhen and the Greater Bay Area, according to Wong.
The park was “making it easier for talent, goods, capital, and data to move across the border”, Wong said.
Lenovo said that its new AI lab had attracted more than 30 partners, including Chinese cybersecurity firms 360 Security Technology and Qi An Xin, server maker H3C and enterprise management systems firm Yonyou.
Lenovo’s addition to HSITP would help attract more companies to set up in Hong Kong to serve global markets, said Vincent Ma, CEO of HSITP.
The Hong Kong side of the Hetao tech hub officially commenced operations in December. Some 60 enterprises had already moved into its two first-phase buildings, representing an 80 per cent occupancy rate. It complements a 302-hectare site on the Shenzhen side.
The city has high hopes for HSITP, which forms part of the Northern Metropolis, a flagship initiative aimed at strengthening Hong Kong’s economic base and deepening integration with the Greater Bay Area. Beijing’s newly unveiled 15th five-year plan urged development of the project.
The Hetao park in Hong Kong aimed to become a “world-class innovation powerhouse”, said Secretary for Innovation, Technology and Industry Sun Dong.
The Asian financial hub has in recent years set about revamping itself as an innovation and technology centre.
The city has started construction on a major supercomputing data centre, which Sun unveiled last June. It is set to provide 180,000 petaflops of computing power, a 36-fold increase from the current level.

data.title: China’s low-cost EVs to be fitted with lidar systems, usually saved for lux cars
data.description:
China’s electric vehicle (EV) makers could introduce a lidar sensor-based intelligent driving system in models priced as low as 60,000 yuan (US$8,795) this year, reflecting intensifying competition and falling costs, according to AI-driven tech firm Robosense.
This price point is the lowest for models on sale in China’s market, excluding mini vehicles sold for less than 50,000 yuan, indicating fierce competition among Chinese carmakers amid the phasing out of policy incentives.
Notably, light detection and ranging (lidar) systems were historically reserved for high-end models or premium trims, as they are a cornerstone of advanced automotive safety due to their ability to provide precise road data during extreme conditions, such as heavy fog. This exclusivity was largely driven by the high manufacturing costs of components.
However, the landscape has shifted dramatically due to rapid technological advancements and the efficiencies of large-scale production.
The average selling price (ASP) of automotive lidar sensors – which sat at around US$80,000 in 2016 – plummeted to US$200 by 2025. This transition has also seen a shift in global leadership, with Chinese manufacturers overtaking US firms as the dominant market players.
By comparison, the lidar units used by companies in the United States range from US$1,000 to US$75,000.
Manufacturers are increasingly integrating lidar into budget-friendly models amid an aggressive price war in the Chinese EV sector. While these systems were seen in vehicles priced between 100,000 and 200,000 yuan in 2025, the floor has continued to drop.
This was evidenced in March 2026, when Leapmotor launched a new model equipped with the technology priced at just 86,800 yuan.


Current trends suggest the threshold is set to drop further to the 60,000 yuan mark, as a wave of Chinese EV makers are expected to soon announce the adoption of a lidar sensor solution as a standard feature for all models of some series, said Mark Qiu Chunchao, CEO of RoboSense.
Qiu predicted that RoboSense’s latest self-developed Phoenix chipset – which allows the lidar sensors to detect items as far as 600 meters (1,970 feet) away – could be used in 60,000-yuan EVs within two years. He did not reveal the product’s selling price.
“To be honest, I don’t really care whether ASP is going to plummet; I just care about how big this pie can get,” Qiu told the South China Morning Post.
He believed that scaling up sales could offset the decline in gross profit margins and that technological upgrades would result in lower costs.
Inexpensive EVs are growing more popular in mainland China. Last year, models priced below 80,000 yuan recorded a sales volume of 1.53 million units and those priced between 80,000 yuan and 100,000 yuan logged 1.49 million units, up by 51.8 per cent and 78.4 per cent year-on-year, respectively.
However, growth across all price bands also rose by 28.2 per cent, data from the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers showed.
Competition among Chinese lidar makers is intensifying, with all three industry leaders – Huawei Technologies, Hesai Group and RoboSense – unveiling breakthrough hardware within a seven-week window.
Over a span of four days, both Hesai and RoboSense announced they would launch their colour-capable lidar sensor, which makes the component work more like a camera while carrying more data.

data.title: Hong Kong, Malaysia exchanges to team up on ETFs, listings, Islamic finance
data.description:
The bourse operators of Hong Kong and Malaysia are exploring a range of collaborations, from dual listings and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) to Islamic finance, to forge closer ties between the city and the Southeast Asian country.
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) CEO Bonnie Chan Yiting and Boursa Malaysia CEO Dato Fad’l Mohamed unveiled their joint development plans in a media briefing in Hong Kong on Wednesday as HKEX hosted the 40th Asian and Oceanian Stock Exchanges Federation (AOSEF) General Assembly at HKEX Connect Hall in Exchange Square in Central.
In the first major collaboration with Boursa Malaysia, HKEX has granted Da Cheng International Asset Management a licence to issue an ETF based on the HKEX Bursa Malaysia Large Cap Index, which tracks scores of the largest companies listed in Hong Kong and Malaysia, with 62 per cent Hong Kong stocks and 38 per cent Malaysian shares.
“This development underscores the growing demand from investors for cross-border products and diversified regional exposure and highlights how collaboration between exchanges can directly support market innovation,” Chan said.
The ETF will be launched and listed in Hong Kong after regulatory approval, and mainland investors will be able to invest in the fund via the ETF Connect scheme.
Mohamed said the two exchanges could also collaborate on dual listings, as Malaysia would welcome listings by Hong Kong and mainland firms. About 30 Malaysian companies were currently listed in Hong Kong, accounting for almost a third of 103 Southeast Asian issuers, HKEX data showed. The biggest player is Guoco Group, which is part of the Hong Leong Group, a Malaysian conglomerate.
“It will be a win-win for both sides, because it helps companies that are already present in markets such as Hong Kong and mainland China to be able to tap into liquidity pools in Malaysia,” he said. “It also gives an opportunity for Malaysian companies that are already listed on Bursa Malaysia to be able to have a secondary listing in Hong Kong.”
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po said HKEX had agreements with 20 exchanges worldwide to facilitate dual listings of equities and bonds.

“Global institutional investors are actively seeking diversification and long-term growth,” he said at the event. “They are looking for Asia’s next generation of enterprises”, including green-energy innovators, digital banks and advanced manufacturers.
“Hong Kong’s role is to ensure that when that capital arrives, it flows efficiently to where Asia’s best opportunities are, wherever they may be listed.”
Mohamed said that collaboration between Malaysia and Hong Kong provided more choice for investors seeking to diversify their investments amid geopolitical uncertainties.
“What we are doing today brings together two complementary markets and marks the start of the journey where investors can view Malaysia and Hong Kong not as stand-alone markets, but as part of a connected Asian market ecosystem,” he said.
HKEX’s Chan said the collaboration would also help Hong Kong develop Islamic finance with support from experts at Bursa Malaysia.
As of January, Malaysia’s Islamic capital market accounted for 2.79 trillion ringgit (US$706 billion), representing 63 per cent of the total Malaysian capital market.
“Bursa Malaysia as a platform that connects companies and sharia-compliant investments with international investors, while serving as a gateway for companies seeking access to [Southeast Asia’s] growth markets,” Mohamed said.
The three-day AOSEF event brings together representatives from 18 exchanges across the region, including Shanghai, Shenzhen, Japan, South Korea, Indonesia and Malaysia.
HKEX chairman Carlson Tong Ka-shing said much had changed since Hong Kong last hosted the meeting 17 years ago.
“The current macro environment is as complex and challenging now as it has ever been in the last couple of decades,” Tong said. “But what stands out is that Asia’s economic presence on the global stage has grown significantly since then.”
Intraregional investment in Asia tripled over the past decade to around US$1.7 trillion in 2024, according to HKEX data.
“With global capital pivoting towards Asia, exchanges across our region have a unique opportunity to strengthen cross-market connectivity and shape the next chapter of robust and sustainable growth,” Tong said.
